title: Gougères
subtitle: Savoury choux buns with Gruyère
excerpt: Savoury choux pastry baked with Gruyère stirred through the dough. They bake hollow and lightly cheesy, crisp outside with a soft, yielding centre. Served warm as an aperitif biscuit or alongside soup.
sourceNotes: Gougères are a traditional Burgundian cheese choux, documented in French household cookery from the 19th century. The combination of choux pastry (pâte à choux) with Gruyère or Comté appears in regional French guides associated with the Burgundy and Franche-Comté regions, where the pastry was served alongside wine as an amuse-bouche. Period recipes appear in French cookery literature that entered the public domain before 1928.

content.text: Gougères use the same choux base as profiteroles with Gruyère folded in at the end. The cheese is added after the eggs, once the dough has cooled enough that it won't melt into grease.
is non-negotiable: close the door and leave it.

Preheat the oven to 200°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Bring the water, butter, and salt to a rolling boil in a saucepan. Remove from the heat and tip in all the flour at once. Beat vigorously with a wooden spoon until the mixture forms a smooth ball that pulls away from the pan. Return to low heat and stir for 1 minute. Transfer to a bowl and cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the eggs one at a time until the dough is smooth, glossy, and drops slowly from the spoon. Fold in the grated Gruyère, pepper, and nutmeg. Transfer to a piping bag with a 1.5 cm plain nozzle. Pipe mounds of about 3 cm diameter onto the trays, leaving 5 cm between each. Dip a finger in water and smooth the tip of each mound. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes without opening the oven until deep golden brown and hollow-sounding when tapped. Pierce each bun with a skewer immediately to release steam. Serve warm.
